cards.title: Wealth Inequality
cards.body: The richest 1% of the global population own more than twice as much wealth as 6.9 billion people. This disparity is a result of various factors, including tax policies and inheritance.

cards.title: Digital Payments
cards.body: More than 2 billion people use mobile wallets, which have reduced transaction costs and increased financial inclusion, particularly in developing countries.

cards.title: Microfinance
cards.body: Microfinance institutions provide small loans to entrepreneurs in developing countries, helping them establish businesses and improve their economic well-being.

cards.title: Cryptocurrency
cards.body: Cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin use blockchain technology to secure transactions and control the creation of new units, making them a decentralized alternative to traditional currencies.

cards.title: Trade Deficits
cards.body: A trade deficit occurs when a country imports more goods and services than it exports, which can lead to a decrease in the value of its currency and impact its economy.

cards.title: Fintech
cards.body: Fintech companies use technology to provide financial services, such as payment processing and lending, making it easier for people to access financial services and manage their money.

cards.title: Central Banks
cards.body: Central banks, such as the Federal Reserve, play a crucial role in maintaining economic stability by setting interest rates, regulating the money supply, and providing liquidity to the financial system.

cards.title: Financial Literacy
cards.body: Financial literacy is the ability to understand and manage personal finances, including budgeting, saving, and investing, which is essential for making informed financial decisions.

cards.title: Stock Market
cards.body: The stock market allows companies to raise capital by issuing shares of stock, and provides investors with opportunities to buy and sell securities, potentially earning returns through dividends and capital appreciation.

cards.title: Taxation
cards.body: Taxes are compulsory payments made by individuals and businesses to governments, which use the revenue to fund public goods and services, such as infrastructure, education, and healthcare.
